allies.rationale_text: Cognitive-load-theory explains why constraints help: boundaries reduce working-memory burden and make focused processing possible.
allies.source_quote: Constraints are the implementation mechanism of CLT.

allies.rationale_text: Checklists turn constraints into explicit operating boundaries so teams can focus on what belongs inside the frame.
allies.source_quote: The primary purpose of identifying and applying constraints is to introduce clarity and consistency.

allies.rationale_text: Simplification helps constraints cut away everything that does not serve the main goal so the signal stays usable.
allies.source_quote: The guiding principle is to Make Everything Earn Its Place.

antagonists.rationale_text: Confirmation-bias turns a useful boundary into a rigid mental cage that resists updating when the frame is wrong.
antagonists.source_quote: Confirmation bias is the enemy of adapting or abandoning an outdated framework, as it resists disconfirming evidence.

antagonists.rationale_text: Anchoring distorts constraints when the initial boundary gets over-weighted after the real problem or environment changes.
antagonists.source_quote: Over-focusing on an initial constraint or piece of information (the anchor) and failing to incorporate subsequent, potentially contradictory or more relevant data.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Constraints conflicts with optionality when rigid constraint adherence prevents exploration of adjacent possibilities that would emerge from loosening a non-binding boundary.
structured_tensions.source_quote: constraints conflicts with optionality (optionality) when rigid constraint adherence prevents exploration of adjacent possibilities that would emerge from loosening a non-binding boundary.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Constraints conflicts with lateral-thinking when imposed constraints channel thinking so narrowly that non-obvious solutions outside the constraint set are never considered.
structured_tensions.source_quote: constraints conflicts with lateral-thinking (lateral thinking) when imposed constraints channel thinking so narrowly that non-obvious solutions outside the constraint set are never considered.

curation_notes.summary: The raw source is strongest on load-reduction allies and conflicts with stale, sticky frames rather than on an explicit tension block.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor relation inflation where the raw source mainly supports bounded allies and two clean anti-bias conflicts.
open_questions: Scaffolding is source-real ally, but cognitive-load-theory, checklists, and simplification were the cleaner bounded anchors into the current graph vocabulary.
